title: FinGPT: Open-Source Financial Large Language Models
model_family: Llama / ChatGLM / others (FinGPT is a recipe applied to multiple bases)
token_budget: Small. LoRA finetuning on curated finance instruction data; not pretraining-scale.
training_stage: SFT (predominantly LoRA); some preference-optimization variants in later releases.
corpus_composition: Financial news (Yahoo Finance, Reuters), sentiment-labeled data (FPB, FiQA), market-data-derived forecasting instructions. News headlines dominate.
input_representation: Base model's native context (4k-8k); no special encoding.
core_mechanism: Attach LoRA adapters to an open foundation model, train on finance instruction-style datasets (sentiment, classification, headline, simple forecasting). 'FinGPT' is a family of models and a data pipeline, not a single weights release. The bet is on cheap adaptation over expensive pretraining.
training_recipe: Single-GPU or small-cluster LoRA. Specific configs published per-base in the repo. Rapidly evolving — base model changes from Llama-2 to Llama-3 to newer over time.
key_empirical_claim: Cheap LoRA finetuning of a strong open base on curated finance instruction data is competitive with from-scratch finance LLMs (BloombergGPT) on most public finance benchmarks at a tiny fraction of the cost.
benchmark_and_metrics: FPB, FiQA SA, Headline, Finance NER; sometimes price-direction forecasting MSE / accuracy. Almost all are classification or shallow QA.
ablation_or_evidence_notes: Comparisons against BloombergGPT and base models often lack strict matched evaluation. Eval contamination is plausible (FPB/FiQA likely in base-model training data). Few real ablations of LoRA rank / data subset / instruction-mix ratio.
foundation_model_potential: Not itself a foundation model. Useful as a recipe for the SFT layer of an in-house FM, not a substitute for it.
why_relevant_for_inhouse_training: Provides reusable instruction-tuning recipes and public finance instruction datasets (sentiment, classification, structured extraction) that can be adapted to our SFT layer. Implication for our plan: for sentiment/classification, LoRA on a strong base is sufficient — meaning the from-scratch pretraining budget should be justified by tasks LoRA can't reach (long-context filings reasoning, novel financial reasoning, agentic tool use).
main_limitations: Most benchmarks are shallow (sentiment, classification). No serious long-context evaluation. No controlled comparison against same-base SFT with general (non-finance) instruction data — so the marginal value of finance SFT vs general SFT is not pinned down.
deployment_risks: Eval contamination (base models likely saw FPB/FiQA); benchmark-bias (sentiment doesn't measure reasoning); base-model attribution confound ('FinGPT-Llama-3 > FinGPT-Llama-2' gain is mostly from Llama-3).
implementation_takeaways: 1) Use the FinGPT repo's instruction datasets and dataloaders as the SFT-layer starting point. 2) For sentiment-style tasks default to LoRA on a strong open base, not from-scratch finance pretraining. 3) Establish stricter eval splits than FinGPT uses (temporal holdout; contamination check).
must_test_hypotheses: H_fg1: A strong general-purpose Instruct model with no finance SFT matches FinGPT-7B on FPB and FiQA. If true, the FinGPT pattern is justified only at base scales where strong general instruct is unavailable. H_fg2: FinGPT-style LoRA does NOT close the gap on long-context filings benchmarks (FinanceBench, LongBench-Finance) — that gap requires continued pretraining or retrieval, not SFT.
open_questions: What's the marginal value of finance SFT on top of strong general instruct? Is there a finance task with a clean 5%+ lift over the base?
